content.content:
1	# Phase 3: VM Server + Memory Management
2	
3	## Context
4	
5	Phase 2 (slices 2.1–2.6, all shipped) gave MINIX 4 a working microkernel: IPC primitives, GICv3+timer scheduling, SVC kernel-call dispatch with a real `SYS_GETINFO`. Today every EL0 process (stubs A, B, C) shares a **single TTBR0**, all page tables are static `.bss` arrays, there is **no physical frame allocator**, and any EL0 translation fault hits `el0_sync_unexpected` and panics. The `servers/vm` crate is an empty stub; `SYS_VMCTL` is one of the 13 ENOSYS placeholders from slice 2.6.
6	
7	Phase 3 delivers the milestone **"Boot processes each have isolated address spaces; VM handles page faults"** — per-process page tables with ARMv8 ASIDs, an EL1 page-fault handler, a real user-space VM server with brk + mmap, and the SYS_VMCTL contract that lets VM mediate paging while the kernel retains all unsafe PTE writes. The POSIX fork and exec calls are deferred to Phase 4 (they require the PM server).
8	
9	**Architecture choices:**
10	- **PTE ownership**: Kernel writes user PTEs. VM passes `(vaddr, phys_frame, prot, asid)` to `SYS_VMCTL(VMCTL_PT_MAP)`; the kernel walks the target proc's page table via HHDM and installs the PTE. Keeps all unsafe paging code in the kernel and avoids VM's self-mapping bootstrap problem.
11	- **ASIDs**: 8-bit ASIDs in `TTBR0_EL1[55:48]` (TCR_EL1.AS left at 0, the Limine default). Allocator panics on overflow; real rollover deferred to Phase 4 when fork actually churns ASIDs.
12	- **Page-table ownership split**: Kernel owns TTBR1 (untouched from Limine). Each proc gets its own TTBR0 root; context switch updates TTBR0_EL1 with ASID-tagged value and ISBs. TLBI is ASID-tagged, never full-flush.
13	- **No allocator in VM**: Region tables are static `[Region; N]` arrays per proc inside VM. `linked_list_allocator` deferred until Phase 4 where PM needs it.
14	- **Kernel reads user memory via HHDM after walking the target proc's page table**, not via `AT S1E0R`. Composes with existing `kernel_va_to_pa` infra and surfaces "VA unmapped" as a clean `Err(EFAULT)`.
15	- **Stubs A/B/C from Phase 2.5/2.6 stay** as regression coverage; they get migrated to per-proc TTBR0s in 3.1b and run through the rest of Phase 3.
16	
17	---
18	
19	## Slice plan (7 PR-sized slices)
20	
21	Each slice independently builds, boots in QEMU `aarch64-unknown-none`, and prints observable progress. Same cadence as Phase 2. Update `docs/plan.md` slice-status markers (`◀ next` / `◀ ready` / `✓ shipped`) as part of each PR.
22	
23	### Slice 3.1a — Physical frame allocator + addrspace API (kernel-only, no EL0 changes)
24	
25	- New `kernel/src/mm/mod.rs` + `mm/frame.rs`: bitmap frame allocator over Limine memmap usable regions. Reserve frames covering: kernel image (`_kern_start` … `_kern_end`), the embedded `.boot_image` section, the existing static stub code/stack pages from Phase 2.5/2.6 (mark all as in-use at boot so they aren't double-allocated). `alloc_frame -> Option<PhysFrame>`, `free_frame(PhysFrame)`, `reserved_range(start, end)`.
26	- New `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/addrspace.rs`: `AddrSpace::new -> Result<AddrSpace>` allocates an L0 frame and zeros it via HHDM. `map_page(va, pa, prot)` walks/allocates intermediate L1/L2/L3 tables on demand using `alloc_frame`, writes the leaf PTE. `walk_pt(va) -> Option<PhysAddr>` for kernel-side reads later. `destroy` walks all live mappings, frees intermediate tables (freeing leaf frames is the caller's job — leaves room for shared/CoW pages later).
27	- `kmain` smoke test: after `proc::init`, build a throwaway addrspace, map 4 distinct pages, walk each, destroy it. Trace `[mm] frame_alloc OK / map OK / walk OK / free OK`.
28	- No `Proc` field changes yet, no userland changes, no IPC changes.
29	- **Files**: new `kernel/src/mm/{mod,frame}.rs`, new `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/addrspace.rs`, additions to `kernel/src/main.rs` (smoke test gated on a `cfg(feature = "mm_smoke")` or just inlined for the slice and removed in 3.1b).
30	- **Verification**: QEMU trace shows the smoke-test sequence and clean boot; A/B/C ping-pong + stub C kernel-call unchanged.
31	
32	### Slice 3.1b — Per-process TTBR0s + ASIDs + minimal pf-diagnostic handler
33	
34	- Add `Proc::ttbr0_pa: PhysAddr` and `Proc::asid: u8`. Add ASID allocator (8-bit, panic on overflow, single-threaded boot invariant documented).
35	- Rewrite `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs`: each stub gets its own `AddrSpace`, its own freshly allocated code+stack frames, freshly mapped. Static `L0_TABLE`/`L1_TABLE`/`L2_TABLE`/`L3_CODE_TABLE`/`L3_STACK_TABLE` arrays are removed.
36	- Context switch (`el1_return_to_user` tail in `trap.S` / `sched::schedule_next` in `proc/sched.rs`): before ERET, write `(ttbr0_pa | (asid as u64) << 48)` into `TTBR0_EL1`, ISB. ASID-tagged TLBI on first switch into a freshly created addrspace. Document and assert that `TTBR1_EL1` is never written (Limine owns it).
37	- Replace `el0_sync_unexpected` panic for EC=0x20 (instr abort lower EL) and EC=0x24 (data abort lower EL) with a one-page `do_page_fault_stub` that decodes ESR_EL1/FAR_EL1/ELR_EL1 + the faulting proc's name and panics with a clean dump. The real handler lands in 3.2; this slice just trades a generic panic for a useful diagnostic so 3.1b debugging is tractable.
38	- IPC user-copy in `ipc/message.rs` keeps `read_volatile(deliver_msg_vir)` unchanged — the SVC handler runs in caller context with caller's TTBR0 live, so single-AS reads still work. Cross-AS redesign lands in 3.4 when VM exists.
39	- **Files**: `kernel/src/proc/proc_struct.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs` (rewrite), `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/trap.S`, new `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/asid.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/exception.rs`.
40	- **Verification**: One-shot debug dump at first schedule shows distinct `TTBR0_EL1`/ASID per proc (A=asid1, B=asid2, C=asid3). Existing ping-pong + SYS_GETINFO traces unchanged. Inject a deliberate unmapped EL0 access (temporary debug stub) and see the new diagnostic instead of the generic `el0_sync_unexpected`; remove the deliberate fault before merging.
41	
42	### Slice 3.2 — Real page-fault handler + RTS_PAGEFAULT (kernel-resolved)
43	
44	- Promote the 3.1b diagnostic into `do_page_fault(esr, far, elr)`: classify (translation vs permission), set caller's `RTS_PAGEFAULT`, record `(addr, flags, ip)` in a new `Proc::page_fault_state` field, dequeue from runqueue using the slice-2.5 `rts_set` helper (capture-then-borrow-end NLL pattern), call `reschedule`.
45	- For this slice, the kernel resolves itself: each proc has a "heap window" range stored in `Proc` (set by userland.rs at boot — e.g., `[0x0100_0000, 0x0100_4000)`). If `far` falls in that window, kernel allocates a frame via 3.1a's allocator, maps it RW+NX via the proc's addrspace, ASID-tagged TLBI, clears `RTS_PAGEFAULT` via `rts_unset`, requeues. Otherwise hand off to the panic path (real SIGSEGV is Phase 4 PM work).
46	- Page-fault flow runs **outside** allocator-from-fault-context concern: the handler enqueues and returns to the scheduler before any allocation; allocation happens when the proc (or VM in later slices) is next scheduled. Document this invariant.
47	- Add a 4th EL0 stub D (ProcNr 14, Priv slot 19, code at 0x40_0000-aligned VA distinct from A/B/C, heap window 0x0100_0000+): touches an unmapped page in its heap window, loops. Stub D shares one IMAGE entry like C — minimal extra `install_stub_d_priv` helper.
48	- **Files**: `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/exception.rs`, new `kernel/src/proc/page_fault.rs`, `kernel/src/proc/proc_struct.rs` (add `page_fault_state` + `heap_window`), `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs` (stub D + heap window setup), `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/user_stub.S` (stub D blob), new RTS flag in `kernel/src/proc/flags.rs`.
49	- **Verification**: QEMU trace `[pf] proc=D far=0x01000000 → alloc frame=0xXXX, map, retry`; stub D loops without panic. A/B/C unchanged.
50	
51	### Slice 3.3 — Real SYS_VMCTL subcalls, exercised from a stub (no VM yet)
52	
53	- New `kernel/src/system/do_vmctl.rs` replacing the `stubs::do_vmctl` ENOSYS placeholder. Subcalls:
54	  - `VMCTL_PT_MAP(endpoint, vaddr, pa, prot)` — install one PTE in target proc's AS via the 3.1a `map_page` path. Returns OK or EINVAL/ENOMEM.
55	  - `VMCTL_PT_UNMAP(endpoint, vaddr)` — clear one PTE + ASID-tagged TLBI.
56	  - `VMCTL_CLEAR_PAGEFAULT(endpoint)` — `rts_unset(RTS_PAGEFAULT)` on target, requeue.
57	  - `VMCTL_GET_PAGEFAULT(endpoint, &out)` — return `page_fault_state` to caller (only valid while target has `RTS_PAGEFAULT` set).
58	  - `VMCTL_VMINHIBIT_SET / _CLEAR(endpoint)` — block scheduling of target while VM mutates its AS.
59	- `kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs`: add `VMCTL_*` subcall constants. Add a new `NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE3 = 14` (calls list unchanged, just renamed for the next slice's const-assert; keep `NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE2` as an alias for one slice to minimize churn — drop in 3.4). The compile-time `assert!` in `system/mod.rs` updates to the new name.
60	- Switch the 3.2 kernel-resolved heap-window into stub D *itself* calling `SYS_VMCTL(VMCTL_PT_MAP)` to install heap pages it pre-faulted on. This exercises the full SYS_VMCTL surface end-to-end without inventing a "fake VM in SYSTEM". Stub D's priv slot gains `SYS_VMCTL` in its `k_call_mask`.
61	- The kernel-resolved fast path from 3.2 stays in place but is now reachable only from procs *without* `SYS_VMCTL` access — keeps the slice composable.
62	- **Files**: new `kernel/src/system/do_vmctl.rs`, `kernel/src/system/mod.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/user_stub.S` (stub D rewritten to issue SYS_VMCTL), `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs` (stub D priv update).
63	- **Verification**: Since stub D can't run while blocked on RTS_PAGEFAULT, this slice's test pattern is **pre-emptive**: stub D first calls `SYS_VMCTL(VMCTL_PT_MAP)` to populate its heap, then accesses it. The on-demand fault → VMCTL_GET_PAGEFAULT path is exercised by VM in 3.4. Trace shows `[ksys VMCTL_PT_MAP] proc=D va=0x01000000 pa=0xXXX result=0` then successful access.
64	
65	### Slice 3.4 — Real VM server boots, ELF loader, cross-AS IPC delivery
66	
67	- `servers/vm/src/main.rs`: minimal sef-style receive loop. Handles `VM_PAGEFAULT` only (mmap/brk in 3.5/3.6). On `VM_PAGEFAULT`: read `(far, flags)` from message, allocate a frame, `SYS_VMCTL(VMCTL_PT_MAP)`, `SYS_VMCTL(VMCTL_CLEAR_PAGEFAULT)`, loop. Uses `minix-ipc` for IPC; no `alloc` crate dependency.
68	- New `kernel/src/boot_image/elf.rs`: minimal aarch64 ELF64 loader sufficient for VM's binary — iterate PHDRs, for each `PT_LOAD` segment allocate frames, copy file bytes via HHDM, zero BSS tail, map into a new `AddrSpace` with prot derived from `p_flags`. No dynamic relocs.
69	- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs`: keep A/B/C. Add a new `vm_bootstrap` path that loads the embedded VM ELF (packed into `.boot_image` via the existing infra), creates VM's AS, allocates a stack frame, sets entry/sp, installs priv slot allowing `RECEIVE ANY` + `SYS_VMCTL` + `SEND` to any proc (for replies). VM gets a known endpoint constant.
70	- New `kernel-shared/src/com.rs` entry: `VM_PROC_NR`, `VM_ENDPOINT = boot_endpoint(VM)`.
71	- **Page-fault → VM IPC**: page-fault handler now sends `VM_PAGEFAULT` to `VM_ENDPOINT` on the faulting proc's behalf. New `mini_pf_send` (or extend `mini_notify` path) — kernel-originated SEND that queues `deliver_msg` on VM if VM is `RTS_RECEIVING` from ANY, else marks pending on VM's priv slot. The faulting proc stays blocked on `RTS_PAGEFAULT` until `VMCTL_CLEAR_PAGEFAULT` lands. Spell this out in the design comment.
72	- **Cross-AS IPC user-copy**: replace `read_volatile(deliver_msg_vir)` in `ipc/message.rs` with `walk_pt(target_proc, deliver_msg_vir).ok_or(EFAULT)? + HHDM access`. Now the kernel can deliver a message into a process whose TTBR0 is not currently installed.
73	- Drop the kernel-resolved heap-window fallback from 3.2; all faults now go to VM. Stub D switches back to "fault on access" (no pre-emptive `VMCTL_PT_MAP`); VM resolves.
74	- Drop the `NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE2` alias added in 3.3.
75	- `kernel/build.rs` / boot-image packer: ensure the VM ELF is built before the kernel and embedded into `.boot_image`. Use existing `tools/mkbootimage` infra if present; otherwise extend it.
76	- **Files**: new `servers/vm/src/main.rs`, new `kernel/src/boot_image/elf.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/com.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/exception.rs` (route pf to VM), `kernel/src/ipc/message.rs` (HHDM walk), possibly new `kernel/src/ipc/pf_send.rs`.
77	- **Verification**: Boot trace shows VM proc starting, receiving its first `VM_PAGEFAULT`, calling `SYS_VMCTL(PT_MAP)` + `SYS_VMCTL(CLEAR_PAGEFAULT)`. Stub D's faults loop indefinitely with each one round-tripping kernel→VM→kernel. A/B/C ping-pong + SYS_GETINFO still pass. **Phase 3 milestone reached.**
78	
79	### Slice 3.5 — VM region tracking + VM_BRK
80	
81	- VM gets a static per-proc region table: `static REGIONS: [[Region; 16]; NR_PROCS]` (or wrapped in an UnsafeCell newtype matching the kernel's PROC_TABLE/PRIV_TABLE convention). Each `Region { vaddr_start, vaddr_end, prot, kind: Kind::{Heap, Mmap, Text, Data, Stack} }`.
82	- VM populates each proc's regions on first contact (lazy init at first `VM_PAGEFAULT` for that proc): synthesizes Text/Stack/Heap regions from the proc's boot layout (info read via a new `VM_QUERY_BOOT_LAYOUT` `SYS_GETINFO` subcall, or hardcoded constants matching `userland.rs` for now — keep the indirection minimal).
83	- Page-fault handler in VM: look up `far` in the proc's region table. Heap region → allocate zero frame, install, clear fault. Outside any region → reply with a "VM_FAULT_SIGSEGV" notice (no PM yet, so log + halt the proc).
84	- New `VM_BRK(new_break)` IPC message handled by VM: extend the heap region's `vaddr_end` up to `new_break` (no eager mapping — pages fault in on first touch).
85	- Stub D switched to issue `VM_BRK` then touch new bytes.
86	- **Files**: `servers/vm/src/main.rs`, new `servers/vm/src/region.rs`, new `servers/vm/src/fault.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs` (add `VM_BRK`).
87	- **Verification**: Trace shows stub D's `VM_BRK` growing the heap region from `0x0100_4000 → 0x0100_8000`; subsequent writes inside the new range succeed, writes outside log the SIGSEGV path.
88	
89	### Slice 3.6 — VM mmap + Phase 3 cleanup
90	
91	- `VM_MMAP(addr_hint, length, prot, flags)` and `VM_MUNMAP(addr, length)` IPC messages — anonymous mappings only (file-backed is Phase 5 with VFS). Place at a hint or scan the gap between `[MMAP_BASE = 0x4000_0000, STACK_BASE)` for free space; allocate a region; faults populate frames lazily.
92	- Stub D mmaps a region and writes to it.
93	- `SYS_VMCTL` subcall coverage assertion: `const _: () = assert!(NR_VMCTL_SUBCALLS == 6);` (or whatever the final count is) next to the dispatch match in `system/do_vmctl.rs`.
94	- Stubs A/B/C documented as regression coverage in `docs/plan.md` Phase 3 entry; no deletion.
95	- `docs/plan.md`: flip each Phase 3 slice marker (`◀ next` / `◀ ready` / `✓ shipped`) inline with PR merges, then write a Phase-3-complete aggregate paragraph at the bottom mirroring the Phase 2.5/2.6 prose style.
96	- `docs/syscalls.md`: enumerate the new VMCTL subcalls and VM_* call numbers.
97	- `docs/memory-layout.md`: tighten the per-process TTBR0 + ASID section if anything diverged from what's in the doc today.
98	- `CLAUDE.md`: add any Phase 3 conventions worth pinning — at minimum the ASID allocation invariant, the "never write TTBR1" rule, and the "frame allocator must not run in pf context" rule.
99	- **Files**: `servers/vm/src/main.rs`, `servers/vm/src/region.rs`, `servers/vm/src/fault.rs`, new `servers/vm/src/mmap.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs`, `docs/plan.md`, `docs/syscalls.md`, `docs/memory-layout.md`, `CLAUDE.md`.
100	- **Verification**: Stub D mmaps a 4-page region, writes to each page, faults trigger VM, frames allocated, writes succeed. End-of-phase trace shows: A/B ping-pong, C SYS_GETINFO, D VM_BRK + VM_MMAP + on-demand paging — all interleaved with clock ticks, no panics, over an 8s run.
101	
102	---
103	
104	## Critical files & reuse
105	
106	**Existing code to reuse (don't reinvent):**
107	- `kernel/src/proc/sched.rs` `rts_set` / `rts_unset` — slice 2.5's NLL-safe RTS↔run-queue helpers. Use these for `RTS_PAGEFAULT` in 3.2.
108	- `kernel/src/proc/sched.rs` `schedule_next` — already flushes `MF_DELIVERMSG` on every EL1→EL0 transition. The cross-AS IPC redesign in 3.4 changes the body of the flush (HHDM walk instead of in-context volatile write), not the call sites.
109	- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/mmu.rs` MAIR_EL1 / TCR_EL1 invariants and PTE bit constants (`PTE_VALID`, `PTE_AF`, `PTE_PXN`, `PTE_UXN`, `PTE_AP_*`, `PTE_SH_INNER`). The new `addrspace.rs` should import these, not re-derive them.
110	- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs::kernel_va_to_pa` — needed by the frame allocator's reserved-range setup in 3.1a and by HHDM access throughout.
111	- `kernel/src/system/mod.rs` `kernel_call_dispatch` — wrap the new `do_vmctl` into the existing dispatch table, keeping the `k_call_mask` gating and the `assert!(NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE3 == N)` compile-time arm check.
112	- `kernel-shared/src/com.rs` `boot_endpoint(SYSTEM)` — add `VM_PROC_NR` + `boot_endpoint(VM)` alongside.
113	- `kernel/src/ipc/{send,receive}.rs` — the `mini_pf_send` in 3.4 is a small variant of `mini_send` that runs from kernel context (no caller proc) and targets VM; reuse the same RTS_RECEIVING-check / deliver_msg-queue pattern.
114	
115	**Files modified per slice** (summary, not exhaustive):
116	- 3.1a: new `kernel/src/mm/{mod,frame}.rs`, new `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/addrspace.rs`, `kernel/src/main.rs` (smoke test).
117	- 3.1b: `kernel/src/proc/proc_struct.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs` (rewrite), `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/{trap.S,exception.rs}`, new `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/asid.rs`.
118	- 3.2: `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/exception.rs`, new `kernel/src/proc/page_fault.rs`, `kernel/src/proc/{proc_struct,flags}.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/{userland.rs,user_stub.S}`.
119	- 3.3: new `kernel/src/system/do_vmctl.rs`, `kernel/src/system/mod.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/{userland.rs,user_stub.S}`.
120	- 3.4: new `servers/vm/src/main.rs`, new `kernel/src/boot_image/elf.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/{userland.rs,exception.rs}`, `kernel/src/ipc/message.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/com.rs`, possibly `tools/mkbootimage`.
121	- 3.5: `servers/vm/src/main.rs`, new `servers/vm/src/{region,fault}.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs`.
122	- 3.6: `servers/vm/src/{main,region,fault}.rs`, new `servers/vm/src/mmap.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs`, `docs/{plan,syscalls,memory-layout}.md`, `CLAUDE.md`.
123	
124	---
125	
126	## Verification (per slice — same QEMU rhythm as Phase 2)
127	
128	```sh
129	# Build
130	cargo kernel-aarch64
131	
132	# Boot. Kernel runs indefinitely once EL0 starts; timeout is mandatory.
133	# Redirect to a file when grepping — live tail loses lines.
134	timeout 8 cargo run -p minix4-kernel --target aarch64-unknown-none --release > /tmp/phase3.log 2>&1
135	```
136	
137	Each slice's milestone is a grep pattern in `/tmp/phase3.log`:
138	- **3.1a**: `[mm] frame_alloc OK / map OK / walk OK / free OK`
139	- **3.1b**: per-proc `TTBR0_EL1 = 0x...... asid=N` lines for procs A/B/C distinct; ping-pong `do_ipc[N]: call_nr=3 ... result=0` trace from slice 2.5 still flowing
140	- **3.2**: `[pf] proc=D far=0x01000000 → alloc frame=0x........ map retry` then stub D loop
141	- **3.3**: `[ksys VMCTL_PT_MAP] proc=D va=0x01000000 pa=0x........ result=0`
142	- **3.4**: VM proc startup line + `[vm] VM_PAGEFAULT from D far=0x... → PT_MAP + CLEAR_PAGEFAULT` round-trip
143	- **3.5**: VM `[vm] VM_BRK proc=D new_break=0x01008000` then stub D writes succeed
144	- **3.6**: VM `[vm] VM_MMAP proc=D addr=0x40000000 len=0x4000` then stub D writes succeed across all 4 pages
145	
146	Always verify the **regressions from earlier phases** still pass on every slice: A/B ping-pong, C SYS_GETINFO, clock-tick interleaving from slice 2.4. Phase 3 ships when the 8s `/tmp/phase3.log` shows all six families of traces with no panic and no `el0_sync_unexpected`.
147	
148	Host-side unit tests in `kernel-shared` should grow alongside any new types (VMCTL subcall constants, VM_* call numbers, ASID type). `cargo test -p kernel-shared` should stay green every slice.
149

file.content:
# Phase 3: VM Server + Memory Management

## Context

Phase 2 (slices 2.1–2.6, all shipped) gave MINIX 4 a working microkernel: IPC primitives, GICv3+timer scheduling, SVC kernel-call dispatch with a real `SYS_GETINFO`. Today every EL0 process (stubs A, B, C) shares a **single TTBR0**, all page tables are static `.bss` arrays, there is **no physical frame allocator**, and any EL0 translation fault hits `el0_sync_unexpected` and panics. The `servers/vm` crate is an empty stub; `SYS_VMCTL` is one of the 13 ENOSYS placeholders from slice 2.6.

Phase 3 delivers the milestone **"Boot processes each have isolated address spaces; VM handles page faults"** — per-process page tables with ARMv8 ASIDs, an EL1 page-fault handler, a real user-space VM server with brk + mmap, and the SYS_VMCTL contract that lets VM mediate paging while the kernel retains all unsafe PTE writes. The POSIX fork and exec calls are deferred to Phase 4 (they require the PM server).

**Architecture choices:**
- **PTE ownership**: Kernel writes user PTEs. VM passes `(vaddr, phys_frame, prot, asid)` to `SYS_VMCTL(VMCTL_PT_MAP)`; the kernel walks the target proc's page table via HHDM and installs the PTE. Keeps all unsafe paging code in the kernel and avoids VM's self-mapping bootstrap problem.
- **ASIDs**: 8-bit ASIDs in `TTBR0_EL1[55:48]` (TCR_EL1.AS left at 0, the Limine default). Allocator panics on overflow; real rollover deferred to Phase 4 when fork actually churns ASIDs.
- **Page-table ownership split**: Kernel owns TTBR1 (untouched from Limine). Each proc gets its own TTBR0 root; context switch updates TTBR0_EL1 with ASID-tagged value and ISBs. TLBI is ASID-tagged, never full-flush.
- **No allocator in VM**: Region tables are static `[Region; N]` arrays per proc inside VM. `linked_list_allocator` deferred until Phase 4 where PM needs it.
- **Kernel reads user memory via HHDM after walking the target proc's page table**, not via `AT S1E0R`. Composes with existing `kernel_va_to_pa` infra and surfaces "VA unmapped" as a clean `Err(EFAULT)`.
- **Stubs A/B/C from Phase 2.5/2.6 stay** as regression coverage; they get migrated to per-proc TTBR0s in 3.1b and run through the rest of Phase 3.

---

## Slice plan (7 PR-sized slices)

Each slice independently builds, boots in QEMU `aarch64-unknown-none`, and prints observable progress. Same cadence as Phase 2. Update `docs/plan.md` slice-status markers (`◀ next` / `◀ ready` / `✓ shipped`) as part of each PR.

### Slice 3.1a — Physical frame allocator + addrspace API (kernel-only, no EL0 changes)

- New `kernel/src/mm/mod.rs` + `mm/frame.rs`: bitmap frame allocator over Limine memmap usable regions. Reserve frames covering: kernel image (`_kern_start` … `_kern_end`), the embedded `.boot_image` section, the existing static stub code/stack pages from Phase 2.5/2.6 (mark all as in-use at boot so they aren't double-allocated). `alloc_frame -> Option<PhysFrame>`, `free_frame(PhysFrame)`, `reserved_range(start, end)`.
- New `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/addrspace.rs`: `AddrSpace::new -> Result<AddrSpace>` allocates an L0 frame and zeros it via HHDM. `map_page(va, pa, prot)` walks/allocates intermediate L1/L2/L3 tables on demand using `alloc_frame`, writes the leaf PTE. `walk_pt(va) -> Option<PhysAddr>` for kernel-side reads later. `destroy` walks all live mappings, frees intermediate tables (freeing leaf frames is the caller's job — leaves room for shared/CoW pages later).
- `kmain` smoke test: after `proc::init`, build a throwaway addrspace, map 4 distinct pages, walk each, destroy it. Trace `[mm] frame_alloc OK / map OK / walk OK / free OK`.
- No `Proc` field changes yet, no userland changes, no IPC changes.
- **Files**: new `kernel/src/mm/{mod,frame}.rs`, new `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/addrspace.rs`, additions to `kernel/src/main.rs` (smoke test gated on a `cfg(feature = "mm_smoke")` or just inlined for the slice and removed in 3.1b).
- **Verification**: QEMU trace shows the smoke-test sequence and clean boot; A/B/C ping-pong + stub C kernel-call unchanged.

### Slice 3.1b — Per-process TTBR0s + ASIDs + minimal pf-diagnostic handler

- Add `Proc::ttbr0_pa: PhysAddr` and `Proc::asid: u8`. Add ASID allocator (8-bit, panic on overflow, single-threaded boot invariant documented).
- Rewrite `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs`: each stub gets its own `AddrSpace`, its own freshly allocated code+stack frames, freshly mapped. Static `L0_TABLE`/`L1_TABLE`/`L2_TABLE`/`L3_CODE_TABLE`/`L3_STACK_TABLE` arrays are removed.
- Context switch (`el1_return_to_user` tail in `trap.S` / `sched::schedule_next` in `proc/sched.rs`): before ERET, write `(ttbr0_pa | (asid as u64) << 48)` into `TTBR0_EL1`, ISB. ASID-tagged TLBI on first switch into a freshly created addrspace. Document and assert that `TTBR1_EL1` is never written (Limine owns it).
- Replace `el0_sync_unexpected` panic for EC=0x20 (instr abort lower EL) and EC=0x24 (data abort lower EL) with a one-page `do_page_fault_stub` that decodes ESR_EL1/FAR_EL1/ELR_EL1 + the faulting proc's name and panics with a clean dump. The real handler lands in 3.2; this slice just trades a generic panic for a useful diagnostic so 3.1b debugging is tractable.
- IPC user-copy in `ipc/message.rs` keeps `read_volatile(deliver_msg_vir)` unchanged — the SVC handler runs in caller context with caller's TTBR0 live, so single-AS reads still work. Cross-AS redesign lands in 3.4 when VM exists.
- **Files**: `kernel/src/proc/proc_struct.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs` (rewrite), `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/trap.S`, new `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/asid.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/exception.rs`.
- **Verification**: One-shot debug dump at first schedule shows distinct `TTBR0_EL1`/ASID per proc (A=asid1, B=asid2, C=asid3). Existing ping-pong + SYS_GETINFO traces unchanged. Inject a deliberate unmapped EL0 access (temporary debug stub) and see the new diagnostic instead of the generic `el0_sync_unexpected`; remove the deliberate fault before merging.

### Slice 3.2 — Real page-fault handler + RTS_PAGEFAULT (kernel-resolved)

- Promote the 3.1b diagnostic into `do_page_fault(esr, far, elr)`: classify (translation vs permission), set caller's `RTS_PAGEFAULT`, record `(addr, flags, ip)` in a new `Proc::page_fault_state` field, dequeue from runqueue using the slice-2.5 `rts_set` helper (capture-then-borrow-end NLL pattern), call `reschedule`.
- For this slice, the kernel resolves itself: each proc has a "heap window" range stored in `Proc` (set by userland.rs at boot — e.g., `[0x0100_0000, 0x0100_4000)`). If `far` falls in that window, kernel allocates a frame via 3.1a's allocator, maps it RW+NX via the proc's addrspace, ASID-tagged TLBI, clears `RTS_PAGEFAULT` via `rts_unset`, requeues. Otherwise hand off to the panic path (real SIGSEGV is Phase 4 PM work).
- Page-fault flow runs **outside** allocator-from-fault-context concern: the handler enqueues and returns to the scheduler before any allocation; allocation happens when the proc (or VM in later slices) is next scheduled. Document this invariant.
- Add a 4th EL0 stub D (ProcNr 14, Priv slot 19, code at 0x40_0000-aligned VA distinct from A/B/C, heap window 0x0100_0000+): touches an unmapped page in its heap window, loops. Stub D shares one IMAGE entry like C — minimal extra `install_stub_d_priv` helper.
- **Files**: `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/exception.rs`, new `kernel/src/proc/page_fault.rs`, `kernel/src/proc/proc_struct.rs` (add `page_fault_state` + `heap_window`), `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs` (stub D + heap window setup), `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/user_stub.S` (stub D blob), new RTS flag in `kernel/src/proc/flags.rs`.
- **Verification**: QEMU trace `[pf] proc=D far=0x01000000 → alloc frame=0xXXX, map, retry`; stub D loops without panic. A/B/C unchanged.

### Slice 3.3 — Real SYS_VMCTL subcalls, exercised from a stub (no VM yet)

- New `kernel/src/system/do_vmctl.rs` replacing the `stubs::do_vmctl` ENOSYS placeholder. Subcalls:
  - `VMCTL_PT_MAP(endpoint, vaddr, pa, prot)` — install one PTE in target proc's AS via the 3.1a `map_page` path. Returns OK or EINVAL/ENOMEM.
  - `VMCTL_PT_UNMAP(endpoint, vaddr)` — clear one PTE + ASID-tagged TLBI.
  - `VMCTL_CLEAR_PAGEFAULT(endpoint)` — `rts_unset(RTS_PAGEFAULT)` on target, requeue.
  - `VMCTL_GET_PAGEFAULT(endpoint, &out)` — return `page_fault_state` to caller (only valid while target has `RTS_PAGEFAULT` set).
  - `VMCTL_VMINHIBIT_SET / _CLEAR(endpoint)` — block scheduling of target while VM mutates its AS.
- `kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs`: add `VMCTL_*` subcall constants. Add a new `NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE3 = 14` (calls list unchanged, just renamed for the next slice's const-assert; keep `NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE2` as an alias for one slice to minimize churn — drop in 3.4). The compile-time `assert!` in `system/mod.rs` updates to the new name.
- Switch the 3.2 kernel-resolved heap-window into stub D *itself* calling `SYS_VMCTL(VMCTL_PT_MAP)` to install heap pages it pre-faulted on. This exercises the full SYS_VMCTL surface end-to-end without inventing a "fake VM in SYSTEM". Stub D's priv slot gains `SYS_VMCTL` in its `k_call_mask`.
- The kernel-resolved fast path from 3.2 stays in place but is now reachable only from procs *without* `SYS_VMCTL` access — keeps the slice composable.
- **Files**: new `kernel/src/system/do_vmctl.rs`, `kernel/src/system/mod.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/user_stub.S` (stub D rewritten to issue SYS_VMCTL), `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs` (stub D priv update).
- **Verification**: Since stub D can't run while blocked on RTS_PAGEFAULT, this slice's test pattern is **pre-emptive**: stub D first calls `SYS_VMCTL(VMCTL_PT_MAP)` to populate its heap, then accesses it. The on-demand fault → VMCTL_GET_PAGEFAULT path is exercised by VM in 3.4. Trace shows `[ksys VMCTL_PT_MAP] proc=D va=0x01000000 pa=0xXXX result=0` then successful access.

### Slice 3.4 — Real VM server boots, ELF loader, cross-AS IPC delivery

- `servers/vm/src/main.rs`: minimal sef-style receive loop. Handles `VM_PAGEFAULT` only (mmap/brk in 3.5/3.6). On `VM_PAGEFAULT`: read `(far, flags)` from message, allocate a frame, `SYS_VMCTL(VMCTL_PT_MAP)`, `SYS_VMCTL(VMCTL_CLEAR_PAGEFAULT)`, loop. Uses `minix-ipc` for IPC; no `alloc` crate dependency.
- New `kernel/src/boot_image/elf.rs`: minimal aarch64 ELF64 loader sufficient for VM's binary — iterate PHDRs, for each `PT_LOAD` segment allocate frames, copy file bytes via HHDM, zero BSS tail, map into a new `AddrSpace` with prot derived from `p_flags`. No dynamic relocs.
- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs`: keep A/B/C. Add a new `vm_bootstrap` path that loads the embedded VM ELF (packed into `.boot_image` via the existing infra), creates VM's AS, allocates a stack frame, sets entry/sp, installs priv slot allowing `RECEIVE ANY` + `SYS_VMCTL` + `SEND` to any proc (for replies). VM gets a known endpoint constant.
- New `kernel-shared/src/com.rs` entry: `VM_PROC_NR`, `VM_ENDPOINT = boot_endpoint(VM)`.
- **Page-fault → VM IPC**: page-fault handler now sends `VM_PAGEFAULT` to `VM_ENDPOINT` on the faulting proc's behalf. New `mini_pf_send` (or extend `mini_notify` path) — kernel-originated SEND that queues `deliver_msg` on VM if VM is `RTS_RECEIVING` from ANY, else marks pending on VM's priv slot. The faulting proc stays blocked on `RTS_PAGEFAULT` until `VMCTL_CLEAR_PAGEFAULT` lands. Spell this out in the design comment.
- **Cross-AS IPC user-copy**: replace `read_volatile(deliver_msg_vir)` in `ipc/message.rs` with `walk_pt(target_proc, deliver_msg_vir).ok_or(EFAULT)? + HHDM access`. Now the kernel can deliver a message into a process whose TTBR0 is not currently installed.
- Drop the kernel-resolved heap-window fallback from 3.2; all faults now go to VM. Stub D switches back to "fault on access" (no pre-emptive `VMCTL_PT_MAP`); VM resolves.
- Drop the `NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE2` alias added in 3.3.
- `kernel/build.rs` / boot-image packer: ensure the VM ELF is built before the kernel and embedded into `.boot_image`. Use existing `tools/mkbootimage` infra if present; otherwise extend it.
- **Files**: new `servers/vm/src/main.rs`, new `kernel/src/boot_image/elf.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/com.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/exception.rs` (route pf to VM), `kernel/src/ipc/message.rs` (HHDM walk), possibly new `kernel/src/ipc/pf_send.rs`.
- **Verification**: Boot trace shows VM proc starting, receiving its first `VM_PAGEFAULT`, calling `SYS_VMCTL(PT_MAP)` + `SYS_VMCTL(CLEAR_PAGEFAULT)`. Stub D's faults loop indefinitely with each one round-tripping kernel→VM→kernel. A/B/C ping-pong + SYS_GETINFO still pass. **Phase 3 milestone reached.**

### Slice 3.5 — VM region tracking + VM_BRK

- VM gets a static per-proc region table: `static REGIONS: [[Region; 16]; NR_PROCS]` (or wrapped in an UnsafeCell newtype matching the kernel's PROC_TABLE/PRIV_TABLE convention). Each `Region { vaddr_start, vaddr_end, prot, kind: Kind::{Heap, Mmap, Text, Data, Stack} }`.
- VM populates each proc's regions on first contact (lazy init at first `VM_PAGEFAULT` for that proc): synthesizes Text/Stack/Heap regions from the proc's boot layout (info read via a new `VM_QUERY_BOOT_LAYOUT` `SYS_GETINFO` subcall, or hardcoded constants matching `userland.rs` for now — keep the indirection minimal).
- Page-fault handler in VM: look up `far` in the proc's region table. Heap region → allocate zero frame, install, clear fault. Outside any region → reply with a "VM_FAULT_SIGSEGV" notice (no PM yet, so log + halt the proc).
- New `VM_BRK(new_break)` IPC message handled by VM: extend the heap region's `vaddr_end` up to `new_break` (no eager mapping — pages fault in on first touch).
- Stub D switched to issue `VM_BRK` then touch new bytes.
- **Files**: `servers/vm/src/main.rs`, new `servers/vm/src/region.rs`, new `servers/vm/src/fault.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs` (add `VM_BRK`).
- **Verification**: Trace shows stub D's `VM_BRK` growing the heap region from `0x0100_4000 → 0x0100_8000`; subsequent writes inside the new range succeed, writes outside log the SIGSEGV path.

### Slice 3.6 — VM mmap + Phase 3 cleanup

- `VM_MMAP(addr_hint, length, prot, flags)` and `VM_MUNMAP(addr, length)` IPC messages — anonymous mappings only (file-backed is Phase 5 with VFS). Place at a hint or scan the gap between `[MMAP_BASE = 0x4000_0000, STACK_BASE)` for free space; allocate a region; faults populate frames lazily.
- Stub D mmaps a region and writes to it.
- `SYS_VMCTL` subcall coverage assertion: `const _: () = assert!(NR_VMCTL_SUBCALLS == 6);` (or whatever the final count is) next to the dispatch match in `system/do_vmctl.rs`.
- Stubs A/B/C documented as regression coverage in `docs/plan.md` Phase 3 entry; no deletion.
- `docs/plan.md`: flip each Phase 3 slice marker (`◀ next` / `◀ ready` / `✓ shipped`) inline with PR merges, then write a Phase-3-complete aggregate paragraph at the bottom mirroring the Phase 2.5/2.6 prose style.
- `docs/syscalls.md`: enumerate the new VMCTL subcalls and VM_* call numbers.
- `docs/memory-layout.md`: tighten the per-process TTBR0 + ASID section if anything diverged from what's in the doc today.
- `CLAUDE.md`: add any Phase 3 conventions worth pinning — at minimum the ASID allocation invariant, the "never write TTBR1" rule, and the "frame allocator must not run in pf context" rule.
- **Files**: `servers/vm/src/main.rs`, `servers/vm/src/region.rs`, `servers/vm/src/fault.rs`, new `servers/vm/src/mmap.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs`, `docs/plan.md`, `docs/syscalls.md`, `docs/memory-layout.md`, `CLAUDE.md`.
- **Verification**: Stub D mmaps a 4-page region, writes to each page, faults trigger VM, frames allocated, writes succeed. End-of-phase trace shows: A/B ping-pong, C SYS_GETINFO, D VM_BRK + VM_MMAP + on-demand paging — all interleaved with clock ticks, no panics, over an 8s run.

---

## Critical files & reuse

**Existing code to reuse (don't reinvent):**
- `kernel/src/proc/sched.rs` `rts_set` / `rts_unset` — slice 2.5's NLL-safe RTS↔run-queue helpers. Use these for `RTS_PAGEFAULT` in 3.2.
- `kernel/src/proc/sched.rs` `schedule_next` — already flushes `MF_DELIVERMSG` on every EL1→EL0 transition. The cross-AS IPC redesign in 3.4 changes the body of the flush (HHDM walk instead of in-context volatile write), not the call sites.
- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/mmu.rs` MAIR_EL1 / TCR_EL1 invariants and PTE bit constants (`PTE_VALID`, `PTE_AF`, `PTE_PXN`, `PTE_UXN`, `PTE_AP_*`, `PTE_SH_INNER`). The new `addrspace.rs` should import these, not re-derive them.
- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs::kernel_va_to_pa` — needed by the frame allocator's reserved-range setup in 3.1a and by HHDM access throughout.
- `kernel/src/system/mod.rs` `kernel_call_dispatch` — wrap the new `do_vmctl` into the existing dispatch table, keeping the `k_call_mask` gating and the `assert!(NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE3 == N)` compile-time arm check.
- `kernel-shared/src/com.rs` `boot_endpoint(SYSTEM)` — add `VM_PROC_NR` + `boot_endpoint(VM)` alongside.
- `kernel/src/ipc/{send,receive}.rs` — the `mini_pf_send` in 3.4 is a small variant of `mini_send` that runs from kernel context (no caller proc) and targets VM; reuse the same RTS_RECEIVING-check / deliver_msg-queue pattern.

**Files modified per slice** (summary, not exhaustive):
- 3.1a: new `kernel/src/mm/{mod,frame}.rs`, new `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/addrspace.rs`, `kernel/src/main.rs` (smoke test).
- 3.1b: `kernel/src/proc/proc_struct.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs` (rewrite), `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/{trap.S,exception.rs}`, new `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/asid.rs`.
- 3.2: `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/exception.rs`, new `kernel/src/proc/page_fault.rs`, `kernel/src/proc/{proc_struct,flags}.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/{userland.rs,user_stub.S}`.
- 3.3: new `kernel/src/system/do_vmctl.rs`, `kernel/src/system/mod.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/{userland.rs,user_stub.S}`.
- 3.4: new `servers/vm/src/main.rs`, new `kernel/src/boot_image/elf.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/{userland.rs,exception.rs}`, `kernel/src/ipc/message.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/com.rs`, possibly `tools/mkbootimage`.
- 3.5: `servers/vm/src/main.rs`, new `servers/vm/src/{region,fault}.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs`.
- 3.6: `servers/vm/src/{main,region,fault}.rs`, new `servers/vm/src/mmap.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs`, `docs/{plan,syscalls,memory-layout}.md`, `CLAUDE.md`.

---

## Verification (per slice — same QEMU rhythm as Phase 2)

```sh
# Build
cargo kernel-aarch64

# Boot. Kernel runs indefinitely once EL0 starts; timeout is mandatory.
# Redirect to a file when grepping — live tail loses lines.
timeout 8 cargo run -p minix4-kernel --target aarch64-unknown-none --release > /tmp/phase3.log 2>&1
```

Each slice's milestone is a grep pattern in `/tmp/phase3.log`:
- **3.1a**: `[mm] frame_alloc OK / map OK / walk OK / free OK`
- **3.1b**: per-proc `TTBR0_EL1 = 0x...... asid=N` lines for procs A/B/C distinct; ping-pong `do_ipc[N]: call_nr=3 ... result=0` trace from slice 2.5 still flowing
- **3.2**: `[pf] proc=D far=0x01000000 → alloc frame=0x........ map retry` then stub D loop
- **3.3**: `[ksys VMCTL_PT_MAP] proc=D va=0x01000000 pa=0x........ result=0`
- **3.4**: VM proc startup line + `[vm] VM_PAGEFAULT from D far=0x... → PT_MAP + CLEAR_PAGEFAULT` round-trip
- **3.5**: VM `[vm] VM_BRK proc=D new_break=0x01008000` then stub D writes succeed
- **3.6**: VM `[vm] VM_MMAP proc=D addr=0x40000000 len=0x4000` then stub D writes succeed across all 4 pages

Always verify the **regressions from earlier phases** still pass on every slice: A/B ping-pong, C SYS_GETINFO, clock-tick interleaving from slice 2.4. Phase 3 ships when the 8s `/tmp/phase3.log` shows all six families of traces with no panic and no `el0_sync_unexpected`.

Host-side unit tests in `kernel-shared` should grow alongside any new types (VMCTL subcall constants, VM_* call numbers, ASID type). `cargo test -p kernel-shared` should stay green every slice.

content.content:
1	//! Process-table entry.
2	//!
3	//! Mirrors MINIX 3 `kernel/proc.h`'s `struct proc`, with simplifications:
4	//! no SMP fields (no `p_cpu_mask`, no `p_stale_tlb`), no profiling counters,
5	//! no syscall-trace bookkeeping, no live-update bookkeeping. The IPC
6	//! linked-list links use [`Option<ProcNr>`] indices rather than raw pointers
7	//! per the convention in `CLAUDE.md`.
8	
9	use core::sync::atomic::AtomicU32;
10	
11	use minix4_kernel_shared::endpoint::{Endpoint, NONE};
12	use minix4_kernel_shared::message::Message;
13	use minix4_kernel_shared::{PrivId, ProcNr};
14	
15	use crate::arch::ArchRegisterFrame;
16	
17	use super::flags::RTS_SLOT_FREE;
18	
19	/// Maximum length of a process name, including the trailing NUL byte if any.
20	pub const PROC_NAME_LEN: usize = 16;
21	
22	/// One slot in the kernel's process table.
23	#[repr(C)]
24	pub struct Proc {
25	    /// Saved register state — populated on EL0 → EL1 trap (slice 2.3+).
26	    pub regs: ArchRegisterFrame,
27	    /// Index of this slot in the process table.
28	    pub nr: ProcNr,
29	    /// Endpoint (generation | proc-nr) — set when the slot is allocated.
30	    pub endpoint: Endpoint,
31	    /// Privilege-table slot, or `None` if the process has no privileges yet.
32	    pub priv_id: Option<PrivId>,
33	    /// Run-time state bits. Process is runnable iff zero.
34	    pub rts_flags: AtomicU32,
35	    /// Miscellaneous status bits (do not gate scheduling).
36	    pub misc_flags: u32,
37	    /// Current scheduling priority (lower = higher priority).
38	    pub priority: u8,
39	    /// Assigned quantum size (milliseconds).
40	    pub quantum_ms: u32,
41	    /// Quantum remaining (kernel ticks); slice 2.4 wires this up.
42	    pub quantum_left: u64,
43	    /// User-mode time consumed (ticks).
44	    pub user_time: u64,
45	    /// Kernel-mode time consumed (ticks).
46	    pub sys_time: u64,
47	
48	    // ----- IPC state -------------------------------------------------------
49	    /// Head of the queue of processes wanting to send to us.
50	    pub caller_q: Option<ProcNr>,
51	    /// Next process in the receiver's caller queue.
52	    pub q_link: Option<ProcNr>,
53	    /// Endpoint we're trying to RECEIVE from (or `ANY`).
54	    pub getfrom_e: Endpoint,
55	    /// Endpoint we're trying to SEND to.
56	    pub sendto_e: Endpoint,
57	    /// Buffered outgoing message (used while blocked on SEND).
58	    pub send_msg: Message,
59	    /// Message to deliver when we unblock (used with `MF_DELIVERMSG`).
60	    pub deliver_msg: Message,
61	    /// User-space VA where the next delivered IPC message should land.
62	    /// Set when RECEIVE / SENDREC blocks (or its caller arrives mid-flight);
63	    /// consumed by `flush_deliver_msg` on every EL1 → EL0 transition.
64	    pub deliver_msg_vir: u64,
65	
66	    // ----- Run-queue state -------------------------------------------------
67	    /// Next process in the same priority-band run queue, or `None` if last.
68	    /// Mirrors MINIX 3's `p_nextready` but as a [`ProcNr`] index per the
69	    /// no-raw-pointers convention in `CLAUDE.md`.
70	    pub next_ready: Option<ProcNr>,
71	
72	    /// ASCII process name, NUL-padded; first 0 byte terminates.
73	    pub name: [u8; PROC_NAME_LEN],
74	}
75	
76	impl Proc {
77	    /// Empty-slot initializer used to fill the static process table at boot.
78	    ///
79	    /// The slot is marked `RTS_SLOT_FREE` so the scheduler can recognize it
80	    /// as unallocated until [`super::init`] populates the boot-image slots.
81	    pub const EMPTY: Self = Self {
82	        regs: ArchRegisterFrame::EMPTY,
83	        nr: ProcNr::new(0),
84	        endpoint: NONE,
85	        priv_id: None,
86	        rts_flags: AtomicU32::new(RTS_SLOT_FREE),
87	        misc_flags: 0,
88	        priority: 0,
89	        quantum_ms: 0,
90	        quantum_left: 0,
91	        user_time: 0,
92	        sys_time: 0,
93	        caller_q: None,
94	        q_link: None,
95	        getfrom_e: NONE,
96	        sendto_e: NONE,
97	        send_msg: Message {
98	            m_source: 0,
99	            m_type: 0,
100	            payload: [0; 96],
101	        },
102	        deliver_msg: Message {
103	            m_source: 0,
104	            m_type: 0,
105	            payload: [0; 96],
106	        },
107	        deliver_msg_vir: 0,
108	        next_ready: None,
109	        name: [0; PROC_NAME_LEN],
110	    };
111	}
112

1	/*
2	 * MINIX 4 aarch64 EL0 → EL1 trap entry + eret stub.
3	 *
4	 * Used for:
5	 *   - EL0 SVC traps (vector slot 8). `vectors.S` branches here unconditionally
6	 *     for that slot; this stub saves user state into CURRENT_PROC->regs,
7	 *     decodes ESR_EL1, and dispatches either `do_ipc` (EC = SVC64) or the
8	 *     unexpected-sync panic helper.
9	 *   - Returning to EL0. `switch_to_user` and the SVC tail both branch to
10	 *     `el1_return_to_user`, which restores `tpidr_el1`-pointed regs and erets.
11	 *
12	 * Calling convention:
13	 *   - TPIDR_EL1 always holds `&mut CURRENT_PROC->regs` while we are running
14	 *     a user context. `switch_to_user` sets it before the first eret; we
15	 *     never clear it.
16	 *   - Field offsets below must match `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/context.rs`.
17	 *     The .equ block is mirrored from the Rust REGS_*_OFFSET constants; the
18	 *     `const _: () = assert!(size_of::<ArchRegisterFrame> == 272)` canary in
19	 *     context.rs catches drift in the trailing fields.
20	 */
21	
22	    .equ REGS_X_OFFSET,      0
23	    .equ REGS_SP_EL0_OFFSET, 248
24	    .equ REGS_ELR_OFFSET,    256
25	    .equ REGS_SPSR_OFFSET,   264
26	
27	    .equ ESR_EC_SVC64,       0x15
28	
29	    .section .text.trap, "ax", %progbits
30	
31	/*
32	 * el0_64_sync_entry — entry stub for vector slot 8 (EL0 AArch64 sync).
33	 *
34	 * On entry:
35	 *   - EL1h, SP = SP_EL1 (kernel stack) is live and usable.
36	 *   - All EL0 user regs x0..x30 hold caller values, unsaved.
37	 *   - TPIDR_EL1 = &mut CURRENT_PROC->regs.
38	 *   - ESR/ELR/SPSR_EL1 describe the trap.
39	 *
40	 * We spill x29/x30 to the kernel stack to free two scratch registers
41	 * (x29 will be the frame pointer; x30 is reloaded from the stack into a
42	 * scratch reg before being written into the frame). All other user regs
43	 * are saved directly into the frame via stp/str, then SP_EL0 / ELR_EL1 /
44	 * SPSR_EL1 follow. EC is decoded from ESR_EL1; SVC64 → do_ipc, anything
45	 * else → el0_sync_unexpected.
46	 */
47	    .global el0_64_sync_entry
48	    .type el0_64_sync_entry, %function
49	el0_64_sync_entry:
50	    // Free x29/x30 for use as scratch (frame pointer + temporary).
51	    stp     x29, x30, [sp, #-16]!
52	
53	    // x29 = &frame.
54	    mrs     x29, tpidr_el1
55	
56	    // Save x0..x28 into the frame. x29 (frame ptr) cannot be its own base
57	    // and destination simultaneously, so we save the user's x29/x30 below.
58	    stp     x0,  x1,  [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x00]
59	    stp     x2,  x3,  [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x10]
60	    stp     x4,  x5,  [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x20]
61	    stp     x6,  x7,  [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x30]
62	    stp     x8,  x9,  [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x40]
63	    stp     x10, x11, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x50]
64	    stp     x12, x13, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x60]
65	    stp     x14, x15, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x70]
66	    stp     x16, x17, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x80]
67	    stp     x18, x19, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x90]
68	    stp     x20, x21, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xA0]
69	    stp     x22, x23, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xB0]
70	    stp     x24, x25, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xC0]
71	    stp     x26, x27, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xD0]
72	    str     x28,      [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xE0]
73	
74	    // Pop the user's x29 and x30 off the kernel stack into scratch regs
75	    // and store them into the frame.
76	    ldp     x16, x17, [sp], #16
77	    stp     x16, x17, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xE8]
78	
79	    // Save SP_EL0, ELR_EL1, SPSR_EL1.
80	    mrs     x9,  sp_el0
81	    mrs     x10, elr_el1
82	    mrs     x11, spsr_el1
83	    stp     x9,  x10, [x29, #REGS_SP_EL0_OFFSET]
84	    str     x11,      [x29, #REGS_SPSR_OFFSET]
85	
86	    // Decode ESR_EL1 EC and dispatch.
87	    mrs     x12, esr_el1
88	    lsr     x13, x12, #26
89	    cmp     x13, #ESR_EC_SVC64
90	    b.ne    1f
91	
92	    // SVC64 — call do_ipc(&mut frame), then run the slice-2.5 SVC tail
93	    // (schedule the next runnable proc + flush MF_DELIVERMSG into its
94	    // user buffer). The tail may transparently swap TPIDR_EL1 to a
95	    // different proc; the subsequent `el1_return_to_user` reads the new
96	    // TPIDR_EL1 and restores that frame.
97	    mov     x0, x29
98	    bl      do_ipc
99	    bl      el1_svc_tail
100	    b       el1_return_to_user
101	
102	1:  // Non-SVC EL0 sync exception — diagnose and panic.
103	    // Args: el0_sync_unexpected(esr, elr, far).
104	    mov     x0, x12              // esr_el1
105	    mrs     x1, elr_el1
106	    mrs     x2, far_el1
107	    bl      el0_sync_unexpected
108	    // el0_sync_unexpected is -> !; if it ever returns, halt.
109	2:  wfe
110	    b       2b
111	    .size el0_64_sync_entry, . - el0_64_sync_entry
112	
113	/*
114	 * el1_return_to_user — restore TPIDR_EL1-pointed frame and eret.
115	 *
116	 * Used by:
117	 *   - proc::sched::switch_to_user (first entry into EL0).
118	 *   - SVC tail (after do_ipc returns).
119	 *
120	 * Both paths leave TPIDR_EL1 pointing at the frame. x9 stays the base
121	 * throughout; x8 and x9 are restored last (otherwise loading into x9
122	 * would clobber the base).
123	 */
124	    .global el1_return_to_user
125	    .type el1_return_to_user, %function
126	el1_return_to_user:
127	    mrs     x9, tpidr_el1
128	
129	    // System registers first.
130	    ldp     x10, x11, [x9, #REGS_SP_EL0_OFFSET]   // sp_el0, elr_el1
131	    ldr     x12,      [x9, #REGS_SPSR_OFFSET]
132	    msr     sp_el0,   x10
133	    msr     elr_el1,  x11
134	    msr     spsr_el1, x12
135	
136	    // GPRs. Skip x8/x9 here — they'd clobber the base mid-restore.
137	    ldp     x0,  x1,  [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x00]
138	    ldp     x2,  x3,  [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x10]
139	    ldp     x4,  x5,  [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x20]
140	    ldp     x6,  x7,  [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x30]
141	    ldp     x10, x11, [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x50]
142	    ldp     x12, x13, [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x60]
143	    ldp     x14, x15, [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x70]
144	    ldp     x16, x17, [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x80]
145	    ldp     x18, x19, [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x90]
146	    ldp     x20, x21, [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xA0]
147	    ldp     x22, x23, [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xB0]
148	    ldp     x24, x25, [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xC0]
149	    ldp     x26, x27, [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xD0]
150	    ldp     x28, x29, [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xE0]
151	    ldr     x30,      [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xF0]
152	
153	    // Finally x8 and x9 — load x9 last so the base survives until needed.
154	    ldr     x8,       [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x40]
155	    ldr     x9,       [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x48]
156	
157	    eret
158	    .size el1_return_to_user, . - el1_return_to_user

file.content:
/*
 * MINIX 4 aarch64 EL0 → EL1 trap entry + eret stub.
 *
 * Used for:
 *   - EL0 SVC traps (vector slot 8). `vectors.S` branches here unconditionally
 *     for that slot; this stub saves user state into CURRENT_PROC->regs,
 *     decodes ESR_EL1, and dispatches either `do_ipc` (EC = SVC64) or the
 *     unexpected-sync panic helper.
 *   - Returning to EL0. `switch_to_user` and the SVC tail both branch to
 *     `el1_return_to_user`, which restores `tpidr_el1`-pointed regs and erets.
 *
 * Calling convention:
 *   - TPIDR_EL1 always holds `&mut CURRENT_PROC->regs` while we are running
 *     a user context. `switch_to_user` sets it before the first eret; we
 *     never clear it.
 *   - Field offsets below must match `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/context.rs`.
 *     The .equ block is mirrored from the Rust REGS_*_OFFSET constants; the
 *     `const _: () = assert!(size_of::<ArchRegisterFrame> == 272)` canary in
 *     context.rs catches drift in the trailing fields.
 */

    .equ REGS_X_OFFSET,      0
    .equ REGS_SP_EL0_OFFSET, 248
    .equ REGS_ELR_OFFSET,    256
    .equ REGS_SPSR_OFFSET,   264

    .equ ESR_EC_SVC64,       0x15

    .section .text.trap, "ax", %progbits

/*
 * el0_64_sync_entry — entry stub for vector slot 8 (EL0 AArch64 sync).
 *
 * On entry:
 *   - EL1h, SP = SP_EL1 (kernel stack) is live and usable.
 *   - All EL0 user regs x0..x30 hold caller values, unsaved.
 *   - TPIDR_EL1 = &mut CURRENT_PROC->regs.
 *   - ESR/ELR/SPSR_EL1 describe the trap.
 *
 * We spill x29/x30 to the kernel stack to free two scratch registers
 * (x29 will be the frame pointer; x30 is reloaded from the stack into a
 * scratch reg before being written into the frame). All other user regs
 * are saved directly into the frame via stp/str, then SP_EL0 / ELR_EL1 /
 * SPSR_EL1 follow. EC is decoded from ESR_EL1; SVC64 → do_ipc, anything
 * else → el0_sync_unexpected.
 */
    .global el0_64_sync_entry
    .type el0_64_sync_entry, %function
el0_64_sync_entry:
    // Free x29/x30 for use as scratch (frame pointer + temporary).
    stp     x29, x30, [sp, #-16]!

    // x29 = &frame.
    mrs     x29, tpidr_el1

    // Save x0..x28 into the frame. x29 (frame ptr) cannot be its own base
    // and destination simultaneously, so we save the user's x29/x30 below.
    stp     x0,  x1,  [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x00]
    stp     x2,  x3,  [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x10]
    stp     x4,  x5,  [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x20]
    stp     x6,  x7,  [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x30]
    stp     x8,  x9,  [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x40]
    stp     x10, x11, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x50]
    stp     x12, x13, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x60]
    stp     x14, x15, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x70]
    stp     x16, x17, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x80]
    stp     x18, x19, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x90]
    stp     x20, x21, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xA0]
    stp     x22, x23, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xB0]
    stp     x24, x25, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xC0]
    stp     x26, x27, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xD0]
    str     x28,      [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xE0]

    // Pop the user's x29 and x30 off the kernel stack into scratch regs
    // and store them into the frame.
    ldp     x16, x17, [sp], #16
    stp     x16, x17, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xE8]

    // Save SP_EL0, ELR_EL1, SPSR_EL1.
    mrs     x9,  sp_el0
    mrs     x10, elr_el1
    mrs     x11, spsr_el1
    stp     x9,  x10, [x29, #REGS_SP_EL0_OFFSET]
    str     x11,      [x29, #REGS_SPSR_OFFSET]

    // Decode ESR_EL1 EC and dispatch.
    mrs     x12, esr_el1
    lsr     x13, x12, #26
    cmp     x13, #ESR_EC_SVC64
    b.ne    1f

    // SVC64 — call do_ipc(&mut frame), then run the slice-2.5 SVC tail
    // (schedule the next runnable proc + flush MF_DELIVERMSG into its
    // user buffer). The tail may transparently swap TPIDR_EL1 to a
    // different proc; the subsequent `el1_return_to_user` reads the new
    // TPIDR_EL1 and restores that frame.
    mov     x0, x29
    bl      do_ipc
    bl      el1_svc_tail
    b       el1_return_to_user

1:  // Non-SVC EL0 sync exception — diagnose and panic.
    // Args: el0_sync_unexpected(esr, elr, far).
    mov     x0, x12              // esr_el1
    mrs     x1, elr_el1
    mrs     x2, far_el1
    bl      el0_sync_unexpected
    // el0_sync_unexpected is -> !; if it ever returns, halt.
2:  wfe
    b       2b
    .size el0_64_sync_entry, . - el0_64_sync_entry

/*
 * el1_return_to_user — restore TPIDR_EL1-pointed frame and eret.
 *
 * Used by:
 *   - proc::sched::switch_to_user (first entry into EL0).
 *   - SVC tail (after do_ipc returns).
 *
 * Both paths leave TPIDR_EL1 pointing at the frame. x9 stays the base
 * throughout; x8 and x9 are restored last (otherwise loading into x9
 * would clobber the base).
 */
    .global el1_return_to_user
    .type el1_return_to_user, %function
el1_return_to_user:
    mrs     x9, tpidr_el1

    // System registers first.
    ldp     x10, x11, [x9, #REGS_SP_EL0_OFFSET]   // sp_el0, elr_el1
    ldr     x12,      [x9, #REGS_SPSR_OFFSET]
    msr     sp_el0,   x10
    msr     elr_el1,  x11
    msr     spsr_el1, x12

    // GPRs. Skip x8/x9 here — they'd clobber the base mid-restore.
    ldp     x0,  x1,  [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x00]
    ldp     x2,  x3,  [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x10]
    ldp     x4,  x5,  [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x20]
    ldp     x6,  x7,  [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x30]
    ldp     x10, x11, [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x50]
    ldp     x12, x13, [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x60]
    ldp     x14, x15, [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x70]
    ldp     x16, x17, [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x80]
    ldp     x18, x19, [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x90]
    ldp     x20, x21, [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xA0]
    ldp     x22, x23, [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xB0]
    ldp     x24, x25, [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xC0]
    ldp     x26, x27, [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xD0]
    ldp     x28, x29, [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xE0]
    ldr     x30,      [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xF0]

    // Finally x8 and x9 — load x9 last so the base survives until needed.
    ldr     x8,       [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x40]
    ldr     x9,       [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x48]

    eret
    .size el1_return_to_user, . - el1_return_to_user

content.content:
1	//! Kernel memory management — physical frame allocator and the address-space
2	//! API live here. Slice 3.1a introduces both; Phase 2.x code did not need
3	//! either (page tables were static `.bss` arrays shared across all stubs).
4	//!
5	//! The frame allocator owns every 4 KiB frame in regions Limine marks
6	//! `MEMMAP_USABLE`. Frames inside the kernel image, the embedded boot image,
7	//! and the static stub pages from slices 2.5/2.6 live in
8	//! `EXECUTABLE_AND_MODULES` and are therefore never visible to this
9	//! allocator — no explicit reservation needed.
10	//!
11	//! Slice 3.1a runs single-threaded with interrupts masked, like the rest of
12	//! the boot path; the allocator carries the same `unsafe impl Sync` boot
13	//! invariant the [`crate::proc::table`] tables document. Phase 3.2+ will
14	//! resolve page faults *outside* fault context (the handler enqueues, then
15	//! the scheduler runs the resolver), so the allocator never runs from
16	//! within a fault path.
17	
18	pub mod frame;
19	
20	pub use frame::{Frame, FRAME_SIZE, alloc_frame, free_frame, init_from_limine_memmap};
21	
22	use core::cell::UnsafeCell;
23	
24	/// Translate a physical address to its kernel HHDM virtual address.
25	///
26	/// The HHDM offset is captured at `init` time from Limine's response;
27	/// all of `[0, 4 GiB)` is mapped under base revision 2 (see
28	/// `arch::aarch64::limine`). Out-of-range PAs will produce a kernel VA
29	/// that faults on access.
30	pub fn phys_to_hhdm(pa: u64) -> *mut u8 {
31	    (pa + hhdm_offset()) as *mut u8
32	}
33	
34	/// HHDM offset capture. Wrapped in the same `UnsafeCell` + `Sync` newtype
35	/// pattern as `kernel/src/proc/table.rs` and `frame::AllocatorCell`, per
36	/// CLAUDE.md's static-mutable-state convention — `static mut` would be
37	/// inconsistent here and trips Rust 2024 lints.
38	#[repr(transparent)]
39	struct HhdmOffset(UnsafeCell<u64>);
40	// SAFETY: written exactly once at boot before any reader, single-threaded.
41	unsafe impl Sync for HhdmOffset {}
42	
43	static HHDM: HhdmOffset = HhdmOffset(UnsafeCell::new(0));
44	
45	/// Set the HHDM offset captured from Limine. Must be called once during
46	/// boot before any frame allocation.
47	///
48	/// SAFETY: must be called exactly once, single-threaded, before any
49	/// concurrent use of [`phys_to_hhdm`].
50	pub unsafe fn set_hhdm_offset(off: u64) {
51	    // SAFETY: caller's contract — single-threaded boot, single writer.
52	    unsafe { *HHDM.0.get() = off };
53	}
54	
55	pub(crate) fn hhdm_offset() -> u64 {
56	    // SAFETY: written exactly once at boot before any reader; the value is
57	    // a plain `u64` so a torn read is impossible on aarch64.
58	    unsafe { *HHDM.0.get() }
59	}
60

3	 *
4	 * Two PC-relative-free blobs, each copied into its own physical page by
5	 * `userland_bootstrap` (mapped EL0 RX at USER_CODE_VA_{A,B}). The stubs
6	 * exercise all three sync IPC primitives:
7	 *
8	 *   Stub A — sender: SENDREC to endpoint B in a loop, incrementing a
9	 *   counter held in x19 (AAPCS callee-saved; the SVC entry/exit code in
10	 *   trap.S saves and restores all of x0..x30, so x19 survives the trap).
11	 *
12	 *   Stub B — echo server: RECEIVE from ANY, then SEND back to whoever
13	 *   m_source identifies. Mirrors m_type unchanged.
14	 *
15	 *   Stub C — kernel-call client (slice 2.6): SENDREC to the SYSTEM
16	 *   endpoint with m_type = SYS_GETINFO and payload[0..4] = GET_WHOAMI in
17	 *   a loop. The kernel dispatcher writes endpoint, priv-flags, init-flags,
18	 *   and name back into the payload; the reply's m_type is OK (0).
19	 *
20	 * Each stub carves its 104-byte Message buffer out of [SP_EL0 - 104,
21	 * SP_EL0). SP stays 16-byte aligned (104 rounds to nothing modulo 16;
22	 * we never sub more than 104 so the stack top remains usable). The stack
23	 * pages are 4 KiB so we have headroom.
24	 *
25	 * Endpoint constants below are the boot endpoints of slots 11, 12, and
26	 * the SYSTEM kernel task (-2). make_endpoint(0, p) = p & 0x7FFF, so
27	 * SYSTEM at gen 0 = (-2 & 0x7FFF) = 0x7FFE. ANY's encoding is
28	 * `(1 << 14) - 1 = 0x3FFF` (ENDPOINT_SLOT_TOP at generation 0).
29	 */
30	
31	    .equ SEND,         1
32	    .equ RECEIVE,      2
33	    .equ SENDREC,      3
34	    .equ ANY,          0x3FFF
35	    .equ ENDPOINT_A,   11
36	    .equ ENDPOINT_B,   12
37	    .equ ENDPOINT_SYS, 0x7FFE              // boot_endpoint(SYSTEM)
38	    .equ SYS_GETINFO,  0x600               // KERNEL_CALL + 0
39	    .equ GET_WHOAMI,   12
40	    .equ MSG_SIZE,     104                 // sizeof(Message); fits in `mov` imm12
41	
42	/* ---- Stub A: sender ----------------------------------------------------- */
43	
44	    .section .rodata.user_stub_a, "a", %progbits
45	    .balign 4
46	    .global _user_stub_a_start
47	    .type _user_stub_a_start, %object

53	    str     wzr, [x9, #0]                   // m_source — kernel overwrites
54	    str     w19, [x9, #4]                   // m_type   — counter
55	    mov     x0, #ENDPOINT_B
56	    mov     x1, #SENDREC
57	    mov     x2, x9
58	    svc     #0

60	    b       1b
61	    .global _user_stub_a_end
62	_user_stub_a_end:
63	    .size _user_stub_a_start, _user_stub_a_end - _user_stub_a_start
64

/*
 * EL0 stubs for slice 2.5 ping-pong demo.
 *
 * Two PC-relative-free blobs, each copied into its own physical page by
 * `userland_bootstrap` (mapped EL0 RX at USER_CODE_VA_{A,B}). The stubs
 * exercise all three sync IPC primitives:
 *
 *   Stub A — sender: SENDREC to endpoint B in a loop, incrementing a
 *   counter held in x19 (AAPCS callee-saved; the SVC entry/exit code in
 *   trap.S saves and restores all of x0..x30, so x19 survives the trap).
 *
 *   Stub B — echo server: RECEIVE from ANY, then SEND back to whoever
 *   m_source identifies. Mirrors m_type unchanged.
 *
 *   Stub C — kernel-call client (slice 2.6): SENDREC to the SYSTEM
 *   endpoint with m_type = SYS_GETINFO and payload[0..4] = GET_WHOAMI in
 *   a loop. The kernel dispatcher writes endpoint, priv-flags, init-flags,
 *   and name back into the payload; the reply's m_type is OK (0).
 *
 * Each stub carves its 104-byte Message buffer out of [SP_EL0 - 104,
 * SP_EL0). SP stays 16-byte aligned (104 rounds to nothing modulo 16;
 * we never sub more than 104 so the stack top remains usable). The stack
 * pages are 4 KiB so we have headroom.
 *
 * Endpoint constants below are the boot endpoints of slots 11, 12, and
 * the SYSTEM kernel task (-2). make_endpoint(0, p) = p & 0x7FFF, so
 * SYSTEM at gen 0 = (-2 & 0x7FFF) = 0x7FFE. ANY's encoding is
 * `(1 << 14) - 1 = 0x3FFF` (ENDPOINT_SLOT_TOP at generation 0).
 */

    .equ SEND,         1
    .equ RECEIVE,      2
    .equ SENDREC,      3
    .equ ANY,          0x3FFF
    .equ ENDPOINT_A,   11
    .equ ENDPOINT_B,   12
    .equ ENDPOINT_SYS, 0x7FFE              // boot_endpoint(SYSTEM)
    .equ SYS_GETINFO,  0x600               // KERNEL_CALL + 0
    .equ GET_WHOAMI,   12
    .equ MSG_SIZE,     104                 // sizeof(Message); fits in `mov` imm12

/* ---- Stub A: sender ----------------------------------------------------- */

    .section .rodata.user_stub_a, "a", %progbits
    .balign 4
    .global _user_stub_a_start
    .type _user_stub_a_start, %object
_user_stub_a_start:
    mov     x19, #0                         // persistent message counter
1:
    // Build Message at [SP_EL0 - 104, SP_EL0): m_source=0, m_type=counter.
    sub     x9, sp, #MSG_SIZE
    str     wzr, [x9, #0]                   // m_source — kernel overwrites
    str     w19, [x9, #4]                   // m_type   — counter
    mov     x0, #ENDPOINT_B
    mov     x1, #SENDREC
    mov     x2, x9
    svc     #0
    add     x19, x19, #1
    b       1b
    .global _user_stub_a_end
_user_stub_a_end:
    .size _user_stub_a_start, _user_stub_a_end - _user_stub_a_start

/* ---- Stub B: echo server ----------------------------------------------- */

    .section .rodata.user_stub_b, "a", %progbits
    .balign 4
    .global _user_stub_b_start
    .type _user_stub_b_start, %object
_user_stub_b_start:
1:
    // Build Message at [SP_EL0 - 104, SP_EL0). Kernel fills it on RECEIVE.
    sub     x9, sp, #MSG_SIZE
    mov     x0, #ANY
    mov     x1, #RECEIVE
    mov     x2, x9
    svc     #0
    // Reply: read m_source out of the delivered message, SEND back with
    // the same m_type already in [x9 + 4].
    ldr     w10, [x9, #0]                   // m_source (signed Endpoint)
    sxtw    x10, w10
    mov     x0, x10
    mov     x1, #SEND
    mov     x2, x9
    svc     #0
    b       1b
    .global _user_stub_b_end
_user_stub_b_end:
    .size _user_stub_b_start, _user_stub_b_end - _user_stub_b_start

/* ---- Stub C: kernel-call client --------------------------------------- */

    .section .rodata.user_stub_c, "a", %progbits
    .balign 4
    .global _user_stub_c_start
    .type _user_stub_c_start, %object
_user_stub_c_start:
    mov     x19, #0                         // persistent call counter
1:
    // Build the request at [SP_EL0 - 104, SP_EL0):
    //   [+0]   m_source — kernel overwrites
    //   [+4]   m_type    = SYS_GETINFO
    //   [+8]   payload[0..4] = GET_WHOAMI
    sub     x9, sp, #MSG_SIZE
    str     wzr, [x9, #0]
    mov     w10, #SYS_GETINFO
    str     w10, [x9, #4]
    mov     w10, #GET_WHOAMI
    str     w10, [x9, #8]
    mov     x0, #ENDPOINT_SYS
    mov     x1, #SENDREC
    mov     x2, x9
    svc     #0
    add     x19, x19, #1
    b       1b
    .global _user_stub_c_end
_user_stub_c_end:
    .size _user_stub_c_start, _user_stub_c_end - _user_stub_c_start

content.text:
Perfect. Now I have enough information. Let me compile the findings:

---

## Slice 3.1b Exception Diagnostic Decoder — Research Findings

### 1. **`kernel/src/arch/aarch64/exception.rs` — `el0_sync_unexpected` (lines 78–91)**

Current full file quoted at `/Users/kevinbarnard/src/minix-4/kernel/src/arch/aarch64/exception.rs`. The target function:

```rust
#[unsafe(no_mangle)]
extern "C" fn el0_sync_unexpected(esr: u64, elr: u64, far: u64) -> ! {
    let mut uart = Pl011::new();
    let ec = (esr >> 26) & 0x3F;
    let iss = esr & 0xFF_FFFF;
    let _ = writeln!(uart);
    let _ = writeln!(uart, "!!! unexpected EL0 sync exception (not SVC)");
    let _ = writeln!(
        uart,
        "    ESR_EL1  = {esr:#018x}  (EC = {ec:#04x}, ISS = {iss:#08x})",
    );
    let _ = writeln!(uart, "    ELR_EL1  = {elr:#018x}  FAR_EL1  = {far:#018x}");
    panic!("EL0 sync exception: EC={ec:#x} ESR_EL1={esr:#x}");
}
```

**Shape:** Receives raw `(esr, elr, far)` u64 values. Extracts EC (bits 26–31) and ISS (bits 0–23). Already prints ESR, ELR, and FAR. Ready for ISS decoding for EC=0x20 (instruction abort) and EC=0x24 (data abort).

---

### 2. **`kernel/src/arch/aarch64/trap.S` — assembly glue and calling convention**

**Location:** `/Users/kevinbarnard/src/minix-4/kernel/src/arch/aarch64/trap.S`, lines 102–110:

```asm
1:  // Non-SVC EL0 sync exception — diagnose and panic.
    // Args: el0_sync_unexpected(esr, elr, far).
    mov     x0, x12              // esr_el1
    mrs     x1, elr_el1
    mrs     x2, far_el1
    bl      el0_sync_unexpected
    // el0_sync_unexpected is -> !; if it ever returns, halt.
2:  wfe
    b       2b
```

**Key points:**

### 1. **`kernel/src/arch/aarch64/exception.rs` — `el0_sync_unexpected` (lines 78–91)**

```rust
#[unsafe(no_mangle)]
extern "C" fn el0_sync_unexpected(esr: u64, elr: u64, far: u64) -> ! {
    let mut uart = Pl011::new();
    let ec = (esr >> 26) & 0x3F;
    let iss = esr & 0xFF_FFFF;
    let _ = writeln!(uart);
    let _ = writeln!(uart, "!!! unexpected EL0 sync exception (not SVC)");
    let _ = writeln!(
        uart,
        "    ESR_EL1  = {esr:#018x}  (EC = {ec:#04x}, ISS = {iss:#08x})",
    );
    let _ = writeln!(uart, "    ELR_EL1  = {elr:#018x}  FAR_EL1  = {far:#018x}");
    panic!("EL0 sync exception: EC={ec:#x} ESR_EL1={esr:#x}");
}
```

fn user_va_ok(va: u64, len: usize) -> bool {
    if va < core::mem::align_of::<Message>() as u64 {
        return false;
    }
    if va % (core::mem::align_of::<Message>() as u64) != 0 {

4. `quantum_left = entry.quantum_ms as u64`
5. `endpoint = boot_endpoint(entry.nr)`
6. `nr = entry.nr`
7. `name[..]` — copied from `entry.name`
8. `rts_flags` — set to 0 (runnable) or `RTS_NO_PRIV` (blocked)

**Key finding:** `populate_proc` does NOT initialize `ttbr0_pa` or `asid` — both kernel tasks and user boot servers will inherit the `Proc::EMPTY` values (both `0`). No per-proc address space is allocated in slice 2.2–2.5 because `proc::init` runs before `userland_bootstrap` and `AddrSpace` exists only as a smoke-test in slice 3.1a. Slice 3.1b must:
- Create an `AddrSpace` for each of the three user stubs (A, B, C) during or after boot.
- Store the resulting `ttbr0_pa` in each stub's `Proc` entry.
- Leave kernel tasks' `ttbr0_pa` as `0` (or add a flag to distinguish them).

---

### 3. `proc::flags` and `proc::bitmap` — relevant constants

**File:** `/Users/kevinbarnard/src/minix-4/kernel/src/proc/flags.rs`

**Run-time state flags** (`Proc::rts_flags`, lines 31–61):
- `RTS_SLOT_FREE = 0x0_0001` — slot unallocated
- `RTS_PROC_STOP = 0x0_0002` — explicitly stopped
- `RTS_SENDING = 0x0_0004` — blocked on SEND
- `RTS_RECEIVING = 0x0_0008` — blocked on RECEIVE
- `RTS_SIGNALED = 0x0_0010` — signal pending
- `RTS_SIG_PENDING = 0x0_0020` — signal handler active
- `RTS_P_STOP = 0x0_0040` — traced (debugger)
- `RTS_NO_PRIV = 0x0_0080` — awaiting privilege (boot servers only)
- `RTS_NO_ENDPOINT = 0x0_0100` — no valid endpoint
- `RTS_VMINHIBIT = 0x0_0200` — awaiting VM page-table setup (WILL be used by 3.1b)
- `RTS_PAGEFAULT = 0x0_0400` — unhandled page fault (WILL be used by 3.1b diagnostic)
- `RTS_VMREQUEST = 0x0_0800` — VM memory-request originator
- `RTS_VMREQTARGET = 0x0_1000` — VM memory-request target
- `RTS_PREEMPTED = 0x0_4000` — preempted, re-queue front
- `RTS_NO_QUANTUM = 0x0_8000` — quantum exhausted
- `RTS_BOOTINHIBIT = 0x1_0000` — awaiting VM boot setup

**Miscellaneous flags** (`Proc::misc_flags`, lines 70–82):
- `MF_REPLY_PEND = 0x0_0001` — SENDREC reply pending
- `MF_VIRT_TIMER = 0x0_0002` — virtual timer active
- `MF_PROF_TIMER = 0x0_0004` — profile timer active
- `MF_KCALL_RESUME = 0x0_0008` — kernel call in progress
- `MF_DELIVERMSG = 0x0_0040` — pending IPC delivery (slice 2.5+)
- `MF_SIG_DELAY = 0x0_0080` — signal delivery deferred
- `MF_FPU_INITIALIZED = 0x0_1000` — FPU context saved
- `MF_SENDING_FROM_KERNEL = 0x0_2000` — kernel-initiated SEND
- `MF_CONTEXT_SET = 0x0_4000` — register context saved
- `MF_SENDA_VM_MISS = 0x2_0000` — SENDA hit VM miss
- `MF_STEP = 0x4_0000` — single-step (debugger)
- `MF_MSGFAILED = 0x8_0000` — IPC message failed
- `MF_NICED = 0x10_0000` — nice() applied

**Privilege flags** (`Priv::flags`, lines 88–109):
- `PREEMPTIBLE = 0x002`
- `BILLABLE = 0x004`
- `DYN_PRIV_ID = 0x008`
- `SYS_PROC = 0x010`
- `CHECK_IO_PORT = 0x020`
- `CHECK_IRQ = 0x040`
- `CHECK_MEM = 0x080`
- `ROOT_SYS_PROC = 0x100`
- `VM_SYS_PROC = 0x200`
- `RST_SYS_PROC = 0x800`

**Trap masks** (`Priv::trap_mask`, lines 115–122):
- `TSK_T = 0` — kernel tasks (no IPC)
- `CSK_T = 1 << RECEIVE` — constrained kernel tasks (CLOCK, SYSTEM)
- `USR_T = 1 << SENDREC` — user processes (not yet used, reserved)
- `SRV_T = !0` — system servers (all IPC)

**Bitmap helpers** (`proc/bitmap.rs`, lines 25–62):
- `set_sys_bit(map, id)` — set bit `id` in Priv-indexed bitmap
- `get_sys_bit(map, id)` — test bit `id`
- `set_call_bit(map, idx)` — set bit `idx` in call-indexed bitmap
- `get_call_bit(map, idx)` — test bit `idx`

**Slice 3.1b relevance:** Reuse `RTS_VMINHIBIT` (already exists, means "awaiting VM setup") and `RTS_PAGEFAULT` for the diagnostic handler. No new flags needed.

---

### 4. `proc::table` accessor signatures

**File:** `/Users/kevinbarnard/src/minix-4/kernel/src/proc/table.rs`, lines 139–171

```rust
/// SAFETY: caller must ensure no other reference into `PROC_TABLE` exists.
pub(crate) unsafe fn proc_slot_mut(nr: ProcNr) -> Option<&'static mut Proc>

/// SAFETY: caller must ensure no exclusive reference into `PROC_TABLE` exists.
pub(crate) unsafe fn proc_table_ref() -> &'static [Proc; N_PROC_SLOTS]

/// SAFETY: caller must hold single-threaded-boot / IRQ-masked invariant
/// and must not hold any other reference into `PROC_TABLE`.
pub(crate) unsafe fn proc_table_mut_slice() -> &'static mut [Proc; N_PROC_SLOTS]

/// SAFETY: caller must ensure no other reference into `PRIV_TABLE` exists.
pub(crate) unsafe fn priv_slot_mut(id: PrivId) -> Option<&'static mut Priv>
```

All take `unsafe` (document single-threaded invariant) and return `Option<&'static mut T>` or `&'static [T; N]` to avoid stale references. Pattern: unsafe functions with explicit invariant documentation.

---

### 5. Current IPC user-copy site — bounds check and HHDM path

**File:** `/Users/kevinbarnard/src/minix-4/kernel/src/ipc/message.rs`

**`read_volatile` location (line 65):**
```rust
let m = unsafe { core::ptr::read_volatile(va as *const Message) };
```

**Bounds check (lines 38–49):**
```rust
fn user_va_ok(va: u64, len: usize) -> bool {
    if va < core::mem::align_of::<Message>() as u64 {
        return false;
    }
    if va % (core::mem::align_of::<Message>() as u64) != 0 {
        return false;
    }
    match va.checked_add(len as u64) {
        Some(end) => end <= USER_VA_TOP,
        None => false,
    }
}
```

**`USER_VA_TOP` constant (line 29):**
```rust
pub const USER_VA_TOP: u64 = 1 << 48;  // 2^48, set by TCR_EL1.T0SZ = 16
```

**HHDM comment (lines 9–20):**
> Slice 2.5's MMU is a single hand-rolled TTBR0 set up by `arch::aarch64::userland_bootstrap`: one shared code page (now two — one per stub) and per-task stack pages all under `USER_VA_TOP`. Because the same TTBR0 is live across every EL0 task in this slice, reading and writing user-mode VAs from EL1 just walks the active TTBR0; no switch is needed.

**Key finding:** The bounds check (`user_va_ok`) is **caller-VA-agnostic** — it only checks alignment and the absolute `[0, USER_VA_TOP)` window. During slice 2.5, a single shared TTBR0 is live; when reading from `deliver_msg_vir` during a RECEIVE or SENDREC, the caller's TTBR0 is already active (single-threaded SVC context, IRQs masked). In slice 3.1b, when each stub gets its own TTBR0, the bounds check remains valid: if caller sets `deliver_msg_vir` to a stack address in its own VA space, the address is still in `[0, USER_VA_TOP)` and still walks the per-caller TTBR0 when the flush path runs. No modification needed.

---

### 6. `arch::aarch64::mod.rs` — module exports and ASID placement

**File:** `/Users/kevinbarnard/src/minix-4/kernel/src/arch/aarch64/mod.rs`, lines 1–36

**Current public modules and exports:**
```rust

pub mod context;
pub mod exception;
pub mod gic;
pub mod irq;

pub fn init() { … }
pub fn halt() -> ! { … }
pub fn limine_hhdm_offset() -> Option<u64> { … }
pub fn limine_base_revision_supported() -> bool { … }
```

**Recommended placement for new `asid.rs` module:**
Insert as `pub mod asid;` immediately after `pub mod addrspace;` (line 2) — alphabetically consistent and logically grouped with MMU-related modules (`addrspace`, `mmu`). This keeps all address-space and TLB-tagging logic co-located. Export ASID allocation / assignment functions from the new module as needed (e.g., `pub use asid::alloc_asid`).
